lines.greek: ἀντιθέῳ Ὀδυσῆι πάρος ἣν γαῖαν ἱκέσθαι.
ἀλλʼ ὁ μὲν Αἰθίοπας μετεκίαθε τηλόθʼ ἐόντας,
Αἰθίοπας τοὶ διχθὰ δεδαίαται, ἔσχατοι ἀνδρῶν,
οἱ μὲν δυσομένου Ὑπερίονος οἱ δʼ ἀνιόντος,
ἀντιόων ταύρων τε καὶ ἀρνειῶν ἑκατόμβης.
ἔνθʼ ὅ γʼ ἐτέρπετο δαιτὶ παρήμενος· οἱ δὲ δὴ ἄλλοι
Ζηνὸς ἐνὶ μεγάροισιν Ὀλυμπίου ἁθρόοι ἦσαν.
τοῖσι δὲ μύθων ἦρχε πατὴρ ἀνδρῶν τε θεῶν τε·
μνήσατο γὰρ κατὰ θυμὸν ἀμύμονος Αἰγίσθοιο,
τόν ῥʼ Ἀγαμεμνονίδης τηλεκλυτὸς ἔκτανʼ Ὀρέστης·
τοῦ ὅ γʼ ἐπιμνησθεὶς ἔπεʼ ἀθανάτοισι μετηύδα·
ὢ πόποι, οἷον δή νυ θεοὺς βροτοὶ αἰτιόωνται·
ἐξ ἡμέων γάρ φασι κάκʼ ἔμμεναι, οἱ δὲ καὶ αὐτοὶ
σφῇσιν ἀτασθαλίῃσιν ὑπὲρ μόρον ἄλγεʼ ἔχουσιν,
ὡς καὶ νῦν Αἴγισθος ὑπὲρ μόρον Ἀτρεΐδαο
γῆμʼ ἄλοχον μνηστήν, τὸν δʼ ἔκτανε νοστήσαντα,
εἰδὼς αἰπὺν ὄλεθρον, ἐπεὶ πρό οἱ εἴπομεν ἡμεῖς,
Ἑρμείαν πέμψαντες, ἐύσκοπον ἀργεϊφόντην,
μήτʼ αὐτὸν κτείνειν μήτε μνάασθαι ἄκοιτιν·
ἐκ γὰρ Ὀρέσταο τίσις ἔσσεται Ἀτρεΐδαο,

interlinear: against-godlike Odysseus, before his-own land to-reach.
But he indeed the-Ethiopians went-after, far-off being,
the-Ethiopians who in-two are-divided, remotest of-men,
some at-the-setting of-Hyperion, some at-the-rising,
partaking of-bulls and of-rams in-a-hecatomb.
there he was-taking-delight at-the-feast sitting-by; but the others
of-Zeus in the-halls Olympian gathered were.
and-among-them of-words began the-father of-men and of-gods;
for-he-called-to-mind in his-heart blameless Aegisthus,
whom the-son-of-Agamemnon, far-famed, slew, Orestes;
on-him musing, words to-the-immortals he-spoke-among:
'Ah me, how indeed now the-gods mortals blame;
for from-us, they-say, evils come; yet they even themselves
by-their-own recklessnesses, beyond fate, griefs have,
as even now Aegisthus, beyond fate, of-the-son-of-Atreus
married the-wife wedded, and-him he-slew returning,
knowing sheer destruction, since beforehand to-him we-spoke,
Hermes having-sent, the-keen-sighted Argeiphontes,
neither him to-slay nor to-woo the-wife;
for from Orestes vengeance shall-be for-the-son-of-Atreus,
literal: against godlike Odysseus, until he should reach his own land.
But now he had gone after the Ethiopians, who dwell far off—
the Ethiopians, who are sundered in two, the farthest of men,
some where Hyperion sets, and some where he rises—
to receive a hecatomb of bulls and rams.
There he sat and took his joy at the feast; but the other gods
were gathered together in the halls of Olympian Zeus.
And among them the father of men and gods was first to speak;
for in his heart he called to mind noble Aegisthus,
whom far-famed Orestes, Agamemnon's son, had slain.
Thinking on him, he spoke his words among the immortals:
'Well now, how mortals do blame the gods!
For they say that evils come from us; yet they themselves,
by their own reckless folly, have griefs beyond their lot,
just as now Aegisthus, beyond his lot, wedded the wife
of the son of Atreus, and slew him on his return,
though he knew the sheer destruction, since we had warned him,
sending Hermes, the keen-sighted Argeiphontes,
that he neither slay the man nor woo his wife;
for from Orestes shall come vengeance for the son of Atreus,
translations.elizabethan:
Poseidon meanwhile sought the Ethiops out,
who dwell far off and sundered into twain,
the uttermost of men: some where the Sun
goes down, and some where he doth climb the sky;
and there he sat, well pleased, to take his fill
of bulls and rams, a hundred slain in feast.
But in the high Olympian hall of Zeus
the other gods were gathered all as one,
and there the sire of men and gods began;
for in his breast he brooded on the death
of blameless Aegisthus, whom the far-famed hand
of Agamemnon's son Orestes slew.
On him he mused, and to the immortals spake:
"Alas, how mortals lay their guilt on us!
From us, they say, their evils spring; yet they,
by their own folly, past their portioned doom,
draw down their griefs. So this Aegisthus wed,
against all right, the wife of Atreus' son,
and slew the man new-come from war, though warned
of ruin sheer: for we sent Hermes down,
the keen-eyed messenger, to bid him spare
the husband's life nor court the wife. In vain:
from Orestes' hand shall Atreus' house be avenged."
translations.modernist:
Poseidon had gone off to the Ethiopians. They live at the edge of the world, split in two. Some where the sun goes down, some where it comes up. He sat with them at the feast and ate his share of the bulls and rams. A hundred head. He was content.

The other gods met in the hall of Olympian Zeus. Zeus spoke first. He was thinking about Aegisthus, who was dead. Orestes, Agamemnon's son, had killed him. Zeus thought about that and spoke to the gods.

"Look how men blame us. They say their troubles come from us. But they bring their own grief. They go past what is set down for them. Aegisthus did. He took the wife of Atreus' son. He killed the man when he came home. He knew what would happen. We warned him. We sent Hermes. Do not kill the man, we said. Do not take the wife. Orestes will pay you back for Atreus' son."
translations.storybook:
Poseidon had gone away to visit the Ethiopians, who live at the very edges of the world. Some of them live where the sun goes to bed, and some where it wakes up. They were having a grand feast, with a hundred bulls and rams, and Poseidon sat down happily to enjoy it.

While he was away, all the other gods gathered in the great hall of Zeus, high on Mount Olympus. Zeus, the father of gods and men, spoke first, because he was thinking about a wicked man named Aegisthus, who had just been killed by brave Orestes, the son of Agamemnon.

"Oh, dear me," said Zeus, "how people love to blame us gods for everything! They say all their troubles come from us. But so often they make their own troubles, and far worse than they needed to be! Take Aegisthus. We sent our messenger Hermes to warn him: do not marry that man's wife, and do not kill him when he comes home. But Aegisthus would not listen. And now Orestes has paid him back for it, just as we said he would."
translations.vansanders:
Poseidon hated the "godlike" Odysseus straight up until he got home. But at the moment Poseidon was out raging with the Ethiopians (who are all over the place) to pick up a shit ton of bulls and rams. He also had a blast partying, but the other gods were all together up in Dzeus's dope ass house. The father of humans AND gods was also there and was the first to the mic. He was thinking about Aegisthus... the one Agamemnon's boy fucked up (KILLED), named Orestes. So, with Orestes in mind, Big Papa says,

"So why the fuck are humans always blaming us for their dumb shit? They say all this bad shit is OUR fault but they run around like dipshits confused as fuck, bitching and crying, complaining and whining, stepping on their own toes... This idiot Aegisthus married my boy Agamemnon's wife and killed him when he got back. EVEN THOUGH he knew full fuckin well what bullshit was in store because Hermes straight up fucking TOLD HIM what would happen... AKA some badass revenge from Orestes. DUDE... WTF?!"

murray.text: there to receive a hecatomb of bulls and rams, and there he was taking his joy, sitting at the feast; but the other gods were gathered together in the halls of Olympian Zeus. Among them the father of gods and men was first to speak, for in his heart he thought of noble Aegisthus,

whom far-famed Orestes, Agamemnon's son, had slain. Thinking on him he spoke among the immortals, and said: “Look you now, how ready mortals are to blame the gods. It is from us, they say, that evils come, but they even of themselves, through their own blind folly, have sorrows beyond that which is ordained.

Even as now Aegisthus, beyond that which was ordained, took to himself the wedded wife of the son of Atreus, and slew him on his return, though well he knew of sheer destruction, seeing that we spake to him before, sending Hermes, the keen-sighted Argeiphontes, that he should neither slay the man nor woo his wife;

for from Orestes shall come vengeance for the son of Atreus when once he has come to manhood and longs for his own land. So Hermes spoke, but for all his good intent he prevailed not upon the heart of Aegisthus; and now he has paid the full price of all.” Then the goddess, flashing-eyed Athena, answered him:
